candidates.item: Does not allow sending messages to the internet (inbound-only or sandboxed service?)
candidates.why_interesting: Most email services are bidirectional by design — an email provider that explicitly restricts outbound sending to the internet is architecturally unusual and worth probing. It implies a very different security model (receive-only or walled-garden), which is genuinely distinctive if true.
candidates.recency_note: Noted in raw research; not independently verified with a timestamped page

candidates.item: Asks not to be blocklisted, distinguishing themselves from throwaway/disposable email providers — suggests abuse can be handled via IP bans rather than domain blocks
candidates.why_interesting: Proactively lobbying blocklist operators with a specific technical argument ('ban the IP, not our domain') is an unusual and somewhat bold stance for a free public email provider. It signals they are fighting an uphill reputational battle and have thought carefully about how spam ecosystem players perceive them — a quirky, specific position rather than a generic claim.
candidates.recency_note: Noted in raw research; not independently verified with a timestamped page

candidates.item: Explicitly states their emails do NOT bounce and they are NOT a throwaway/disposable email service
candidates.why_interesting: Free public email providers almost universally get lumped in with disposable/temp-mail services by spam filters and deliverability tools. Explicitly differentiating on bounce rate and permanence — and staking a claim that their addresses are real, persistent inboxes — is a specific, falsifiable product stance, not just a marketing slogan.
candidates.recency_note: Noted in raw research; not independently verified with a timestamped page
